audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Numbers / Bemidbar red-cow and corpse-impurity purification text tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for red-cow rite, outside-camp burning, cedar/hyssop/scarlet, ash collection, corpse impurity, tent and grave contact, third/seventh-day sprinkling, and paradoxical impurity of ritual handlers. Anti-stigma and medical-safety safeguards required.

verses.restored: YHWH spoke to Moses and Aaron, saying,

This is statute of Torah/instruction that YHWH commanded, saying: Speak to children of Israel, and let them take to you a red cow, complete, in which there is no defect, upon which no yoke has gone up.
You shall give her to Eleazar the priest, and he shall bring her outside camp, and she shall be slaughtered before him.
Eleazar the priest shall take from her blood with his finger and sprinkle toward face/front of tent of meeting from her blood seven times.
The cow shall be burned before his eyes; her hide, flesh, blood, and dung shall be burned.
The priest shall take cedar wood, hyssop, and scarlet material and cast them into midst of burning cow.
The priest shall wash his garments and bathe his flesh in water; afterward he may enter camp, but priest shall be unclean until evening.
The one burning her shall wash his garments in water and bathe his flesh in water, and he shall be unclean until evening.

A clean man shall gather ashes of cow and deposit them outside camp in clean place; they shall be kept for congregation of children of Israel for water of impurity-removal; it is a sin/purification offering.
The one gathering ashes of cow shall wash his garments and be unclean until evening. It shall be for children of Israel and for sojourner sojourning in their midst as age-long statute.
The one touching dead body of any human soul/life shall be unclean seven days.
He shall purify himself with it on third day and on seventh day he shall be clean; but if he does not purify himself on third day and seventh day, he shall not be clean.

Everyone touching dead body, soul/life of a human who died, and not purifying himself, makes dwelling-place of YHWH unclean; that soul/life shall be cut off from Israel. Because water of impurity-removal was not sprinkled upon him, unclean he shall be; his uncleanness remains upon him.
This is Torah/instruction when a human dies in a tent: everyone entering tent and everyone in tent shall be unclean seven days.
Every open vessel upon which no covering is fastened shall be unclean.
Everyone who in open field touches one slain by sword, or a dead body, or human bone, or grave shall be unclean seven days.
For the unclean they shall take from ashes of burning of sin/purification offering and put living/running water upon them in a vessel.
A clean man shall take hyssop and dip it in water and sprinkle upon tent, all vessels, and persons who were there, and upon one who touched bone, slain one, dead one, or grave.
The clean shall sprinkle upon unclean on third day and seventh day; on seventh day he shall purify him. He shall wash his garments and bathe in water, and at evening he shall be clean.

But a man who becomes unclean and does not purify himself, that soul/life shall be cut off from midst of assembly, because sanctuary of YHWH he made unclean; water of impurity-removal was not sprinkled upon him; unclean he is.
It shall be for them as age-long statute: one sprinkling water of impurity-removal shall wash his garments, and one touching water of impurity-removal shall be unclean until evening.
Whatever unclean one touches shall be unclean, and soul/life touching it shall be unclean until evening.

translation_notes: Corpse impurity is a ritual condition associated with death, not moral guilt, sinfulness of grief, or reduced human worth.
The red-cow rite occurs outside camp and produces ashes mixed with living water for later sprinkling.
Those who prepare or handle the purifying materials become temporarily unclean, creating a deliberate ritual paradox: the agent of purification also bears impurity.
The same statute applies to Israelite and sojourner.
The rite is not modern sanitation, medicine, disinfectant, or evidence-based treatment and must not replace healthcare.
Cut-off language is severe ancient sanctuary-boundary material and must not authorize punishment of mourners or those handling the dead.
safety_note: Do not use Numbers 19 to stigmatize grief, death-care workers, illness, disability, menstruation, or bodies; do not reproduce the animal rite or substitute ritual water for medical care. The chapter regulates ancient sanctuary access after contact with death.
